title: Getting Things Done (GTD)
source: Getting Things Done by David Allen
content:
GTD (Getting Things Done) is a time management and personal productivity method developed by David Allen. Its core premise: your mind is for having ideas, not holding them. When you store incomplete tasks in your head, your brain uses cognitive resources to track them, creating stress and reduced focus.

The GTD Workflow has five stages:

1. CAPTURE: Collect every task, idea, and commitment into a trusted external system (inbox) — not your mind. Use a single capture tool consistently.

2. CLARIFY: Process each captured item by asking: Is it actionable?
   - Not actionable → Delete, Incubate (Someday/Maybe list), or Reference
   - Actionable → Define the next physical action
   - If the action takes less than 2 minutes, do it now (the 2-Minute Rule)
   - If you're not the right person, delegate it
   - Otherwise, defer it

3. ORGANIZE: Place items in the right lists:
   - Next Actions: things to do as soon as possible
   - Projects: any goal requiring more than one action
   - Waiting For: delegated tasks you're tracking
   - Someday/Maybe: ideas to consider in the future
   - Calendar: only date-specific or time-specific actions

4. REFLECT: Review your lists regularly. The Weekly Review is the critical habit: every week, clear inboxes, review projects, update Next Actions, and ensure your system is current and complete.

5. ENGAGE: Simply do the work. With a clear, trusted system, choosing what to work on becomes easier. Use four criteria: context (what tools/location you need), time available, energy available, and priority.

The 2-Minute Rule: If an action will take less than 2 minutes to complete, do it immediately when you capture and clarify it. It takes longer to track it than to do it.

Projects vs. Actions: Any desired outcome requiring more than one action step is a 'project.' Projects need next actions assigned, not just names. A project without a next action is a stalled commitment.

The Natural Planning Model: When planning any project, use the brain's natural planning process: (1) define purpose and principles, (2) visualize the outcome, (3) brainstorm, (4) organize, (5) identify next actions. This is far more effective than reactive 'day planning.'
